---
date: "2026-07-27"
---

## A Text Number Is a Measurement, Not a Fact

**Learning objectives.** By the end you can:

- Treat a text-derived number as a noisy proxy
- Explain how mismeasurement attenuates or biases coefficients
- Validate an instrument with a held-out gold set
- Disclose prompts and labels so referees can judge

::: notes
Welcome to Mentor Session six, part one of five. All week the raw material changed under your feet. For five weeks, the word data meant numbers that arrived already numeric — a return, a leverage ratio, a yield. This week the data was prose: a ten-K running to fifty thousand words, a product description a firm wrote about itself, a risk-factor section a lawyer drafted to be read by no one. Hoberg and Phillips turned product descriptions into a map of who competes with whom. Loughran and McDonald showed that the most popular tool for scoring financial tone was counting the words liability and tax as gloom. And in Chapter six-point-five you met the newest and most dangerous instrument of all — a large language model that will read forty thousand filings overnight and hand back a confident column of labels, some quietly invented. Today we put those three things into one discipline.
:::

## The One Sentence Today Is Built On

- This week, data arrived as **prose**, not numbers
- Prose must be **measured** into a number before any regression
- A text number is a **noisy proxy** for an unseen latent quantity
- An LLM is a fluent instrument — **uncalibrated until you calibrate it**

::: notes
Here is the sentence the whole session rests on. A number you extracted from text is a measurement — a noisy proxy for something you cannot see directly — and a large language model is just a very fluent measuring instrument that has not been calibrated until you calibrate it. There is a seductive illusion in text-as-data, and it gets stronger the better your tools get. You point an instrument at a document, and out comes a number: tone equals minus zero-point-zero-four, similarity equals zero-point-three-one, label equals negative. It looks like a fact about the document, the way the filing date is a fact. It is not. It is the output of an instrument, and like every instrument it has error.
:::

## Back to Week One: the Proxy Has Error

- True latent quantity + **systematic bias** + **random noise**
- Systematic: over-calls "negative" on legal boilerplate
- Random: re-run the LLM, the same filing **flips** its label
- A mismeasured regressor — same idea, brand-new sensor

::: notes
Recall the idea from Week one. When you write down the variable tone, you are not observing the filing's true latent sentiment — you are observing a noisy proxy for it. That proxy is the true thing, plus a systematic bias, plus random noise. The systematic part might be that your instrument consistently over-calls negative on legal boilerplate — it reads gloom that is not really there, every single time. The random part is that if you re-run the LLM, the same filing might flip its label from one run to the next. So the number you record is the truth blurred by both a steady tilt and a jitter. This is not a new kind of magic. It is the exact same measurement error you studied in Week one, now coming from a new sensor.
:::

## What Mismeasurement Does Downstream

- The instant the proxy enters a regression, it is a **mismeasured variable**
- Best case: coefficient **attenuated toward zero**, standard errors inflated
- Worst case: error correlated with the model — **bias you cannot sign**
- So the right question is never "is the LLM accurate?" in the abstract

::: notes
And you already know what mismeasurement does to a regression. The instant that proxy enters as a variable, it is a mismeasured regressor. At best, classical measurement error attenuates your coefficient toward zero and inflates your standard errors. At worst, if the measurement error is correlated with something else in the model, it biases the estimate in a direction you cannot even sign — you do not know whether you are overstating or understating the truth. So the right question is never the abstract one, is the LLM accurate. The right question is sharper: how accurate is this instrument, on my data, against truth — and is my downstream result robust to the error I know is there?
:::

## Key Takeaway

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
A text-derived number is a **noisy proxy** for an unseen latent quantity, not a fact. The moment it enters a regression it is a **mismeasured regressor** — at best attenuated, at worst biased in a direction you cannot sign. The real question is how accurate your instrument is, on your data, against truth.
:::

::: notes
To summarize part one: a number extracted from text is a measurement, not a fact. It is the truth plus a systematic bias plus random noise, and the moment it enters a regression it behaves like any mismeasured regressor — attenuated toward zero at best, biased in an unknowable direction at worst. So the honest question is never whether the LLM is accurate in the abstract, but how accurate it is on your data, against truth, and whether your result survives the error you know is baked in. In part two we line up this week's three instruments from the most transparent to the most opaque, and we build the one non-negotiable shape that validation always takes.
:::
